title: Lattice fermion formulation via Physics-Informed Neural Networks: Ginsparg-Wilson relation and Overlap fermions
summary: We propose a novel, machine-learning-based framework for constructing lattice fermions using Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs). Our approach treats the formulation of the Dirac operator as an optimization problem guided by physical requirements, such as symmetries, locality and doubler-decoupling conditions. We first demonstrate that, when trained to satisfy the Ginsparg-Wilson (GW) relation as a soft constraint, a neural network reproduces the overlap fermion operator to high numerical accuracy and learns an effective sign-function mapping without explicitly using a prescribed polynomial or rational approximation. Secondly, we extend the framework from operator construction to machine-assisted algebraic discovery. Within a generalized polynomial ansatz, the network autonomously drives higher-order terms to zero and recovers the standard Ginsparg-Wilson relation. Remarkably, by changing the initial search bias, the same framework also finds a distinct solution corresponding to a Fujikawa-type generalized GW relation.

AI.tldr: A novel machine-learning-based framework, using Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs), constructs lattice fermions by formulating the Dirac operator as an optimization problem. The method reproduces overlap fermion operators and discovers algebraic solutions to the Ginsparg-Wilson (GW) relation, including a generalized GW relation.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to develop a new approach for constructing lattice fermions that can satisfy physical requirements such as symmetries, locality, and doubler-decoupling conditions through a machine learning technique, specifically using PINNs.
AI.method: The method involves training a neural network to satisfy the Ginsparg-Wilson (GW) relation as a soft constraint, which allows it to reproduce the overlap fermion operator with high accuracy. The framework also extends to machine-assisted algebraic discovery where the network autonomously eliminates higher-order terms within a polynomial ansatz to recover the standard GW relation and even finds a distinct solution related to a Fujikawa-type generalized GW relation.
AI.result: The neural network successfully reproduces the overlap fermion operator and learns an effective sign-function mapping without the need for a prescribed approximation. It also recovers the standard GW relation and discovers a distinct solution corresponding to a Fujikawa-type generalized GW relation.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework demonstrates the potential of machine learning, particularly PINNs, in constructing lattice fermions and discovering algebraic solutions to the GW relation, suggesting a promising direction for future research in lattice field theory.

title: Diffusion model for SU(N) gauge theories
summary: Implicit score matching provides a computationally efficient approach for training diffusion models and generating high-quality samples from complex distributions. In this work, we develop a score-matching framework for SU(N) lattice gauge theories, which can be extended to other Lie groups. We apply the method to SU(3) gauge configurations with the Wilson gauge action in two and four dimensions and assess the quality of the generated samples by comparison with Hybrid Monte Carlo (HMC) simulations. We show that the diffusion models can be successfully trained and applied for sampling the Wilson gauge action. For large values of inverse coupling, accurate reverse-time integration requires predictor-corrector schemes, for which we introduce a corrector based on Hamiltonian molecular dynamics. While the corrector significantly improves sampling quality, it also increases the computational cost. We outline several strategies for improving sampling efficiency.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a score-matching framework for SU(N) lattice gauge theories, successfully training diffusion models to sample the Wilson gauge action. A corrector based on Hamiltonian molecular dynamics is introduced to improve sampling quality, though it increases computational cost, and strategies for enhancing sampling efficiency are discussed.
AI.motivation: The motivation of the paper is to develop an efficient method for training diffusion models and generating high-quality samples from complex distributions, specifically in the context of SU(N) lattice gauge theories. The authors aim to apply this method to SU(3) gauge configurations with the Wilson gauge action and compare the results with Hybrid Monte Carlo (HMC) simulations.
AI.method: The method employed in the paper involves developing a score-matching framework tailored for SU(N) lattice gauge theories, which can be extended to other Lie groups. This framework is then applied to SU(3) gauge configurations in two and four dimensions. For improving the accuracy of reverse-time integration, particularly at large values of inverse coupling, the authors introduce a corrector based on Hamiltonian molecular dynamics.
AI.result: The results show that diffusion models can be effectively trained and used for sampling the Wilson gauge action. The use of a corrector improves the quality of the generated samples, although it comes with an increased computational cost. The comparison with HMC simulations indicates that the proposed method is capable of producing high-quality samples.
AI.conclusion: In conclusion, the paper demonstrates the successful application of a score-matching framework for training diffusion models in the context of SU(N) lattice gauge theories. The introduction of a corrector based on Hamiltonian molecular dynamics enhances the quality of the generated samples, but also highlights the need for further work on improving sampling efficiency.

title: The strange and flavor-singlet axial form factors of the nucleon from lattice QCD
comment: 14 pages, 8 figures, 5 tables; prepared for submission to PRD
summary: The singlet axial form factor of the nucleon provides essential input for a complete understanding of the nucleon axial structure. Together with the isovector and isoscalar octet channels, in the forward limit it forms the basis for a full flavor decomposition of the proton spin. In this work we present a lattice QCD determination of the singlet axial form factor $G^{u+d+s}_A(Q^2)$ and related strange contribution $G^{s}_A(Q^2)$ using a set of $N_f = 2 + 1$ CLS gauge ensembles with $O(a)$-improved Wilson fermions, with a full error budget for the extrapolation to the chiral, continuum and infinite-volume limits. Particular focus is placed on the treatment of the disconnected contributions, which constitute the crucial element for the extraction of the strange component. Together with determinations of the isovector and isoscalar octet axial form factors, this work provides a comprehensive lattice QCD determination of the nucleon axial structure across different flavor channels.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a comprehensive lattice QCD determination of the nucleon's axial structure, focusing on the singlet axial form factor and the strange quark contribution, using $N_f = 2 + 1$ CLS gauge ensembles with improved Wilson fermions. The study includes a full error analysis for extrapolations to physical conditions and highlights the treatment of disconnected contributions.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to provide essential input for understanding the nucleon's axial structure, including a full flavor decomposition of the proton spin, which requires knowledge of the singlet axial form factor and the strange quark's contribution.
AI.method: The method involves using lattice QCD with $N_f = 2 + 1$ CLS gauge ensembles and $O(a)$-improved Wilson fermions to determine the singlet axial form factor $G^{u+d+s}_A(Q^2)$ and the strange contribution $G^{s}_A(Q^2)$. The research also carefully treats the disconnected contributions, which are key to extracting the strange component, and performs a full error budget for extrapolating to the chiral, continuum, and infinite-volume limits.
AI.result: The results include a detailed determination of the singlet axial form factor and the related strange contribution, providing a complete picture of the nucleon's axial structure across different flavor channels, with a particular emphasis on the accuracy of the strange quark's role in the nucleon's axial properties.
AI.conclusion: The conclusion is that this work offers a thorough and accurate lattice QCD calculation of the nucleon's axial form factors, contributing significantly to the understanding of the nucleon's axial structure and the flavor decomposition of the proton spin, especially by clarifying the strange quark's role.

title: $F_K/F_π$ as a precision test of a new four flavor Domain Wall Fermion action
summary: We present a new set of lattice QCD ensembles with four flavors of smeared Möbius Domain Wall Fermions with good chiral symmetry and small fifth-dimensional extent. A modest amount of computing resources was sufficient to generate 30 publicly-available ensembles spanning five lattice spacings and a broad range of pion masses down to physical. To scrutinize our action we determine $F_{K^{\pm}}/F_{π^{\pm}} = 1.1962(34)$, a key quantity for precision CKM unitarity tests, heralding a future of inexpensive high-precision calculations of hadronic observables with chiral fermions.
